sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable games from the year, led by month-specific anchors when the evidence supports them.

items.title: Sonic & Knuckles
items.body: Sega's lock-on cartridge turns hardware packaging into part of the trick.
items.sourceNote: October 1994 release context is widely cited.

items.title: Sonic the Hedgehog 3
items.body: Sega's mascot returns with richer zones and a locked-on future ahead.
items.sourceNote: February 1994 release context is widely cited.

items.title: Super Metroid
items.body: Nintendo builds a dense, lonely action map that feels designed for memory.
items.sourceNote: Japanese March and North American April timing is commonly cited.

items.title: Final Fantasy VI
items.body: Square delivers a sweeping late-16-bit RPG ensemble.
items.sourceNote: Japanese release date is widely cited.

items.title: Doom II
items.body: id's sequel turns Doom into a boxed retail event as well as a PC obsession.
items.sourceNote: September 1994 release is widely cited.

items.title: Donkey Kong Country
items.body: Rare and Nintendo make pre-rendered graphics into a Christmas retail weapon.
items.sourceNote: November 1994 release is widely cited.

items.title: Warcraft: Orcs & Humans
items.body: Blizzard begins a fantasy RTS language of workers, barracks and fog of war.
items.sourceNote: November 1994 release is commonly cited.

items.title: Ridge Racer
items.body: Namco brings arcade-style 3D racing to Sony's Japanese launch moment.
items.sourceNote: Japanese PlayStation launch context.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Machines, formats and buying context around the exhibit month.

items.title: Sega Saturn launches in Japan
items.body: Sega enters the 32-bit generation with Virtua Fighter as its prestige argument.
items.sourceNote: November 22, 1994 Japanese launch is widely cited.

items.title: PlayStation launches in Japan
items.body: Sony moves from electronics giant to console platform holder.
items.sourceNote: December 3, 1994 Japanese launch is widely cited.

items.title: 32X complicates Sega's message
items.body: The add-on promises an upgrade path just as Saturn is arriving elsewhere.
items.sourceNote: Late 1994/early 1995 regional context.

items.title: SNES still has visual theatre left
items.body: Donkey Kong Country proves the old cartridge machine can still surprise the shop window.
items.sourceNote: November 1994 release and UK sales context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How players found information before search, streams and social feeds.

items.title: Doom II keeps PC networks busy
items.body: The sequel gives shareware-era players another reason to connect machines and trade WAD talk.

items.title: Usenet and FTP feel like back rooms
items.body: The public web exists, but most game knowledge is still gathered through rougher online spaces.

items.title: Patch culture becomes normal on PC
items.body: Big PC games make updates, drivers and troubleshooting feel like part of ownership.

items.title: Magazine discs bridge offline and online
items.body: A UK coverdisc can still do what a download would later do instantly.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month from the player side of history.

items.title: The next generation was visible but remote
items.body: Saturn and PlayStation existed in Japan first, glowing through import news and magazine previews.

items.title: Cartridges fought back
items.body: Donkey Kong Country made the SNES look impossible for a moment.

items.title: PC games felt bigger and fussier
items.body: Warcraft, X-COM and Doom II rewarded patience, manuals and hardware confidence.

items.title: Buying felt like choosing a future
items.body: 32X, Mega-CD, Saturn, PlayStation and old consoles made every shop-window decision feel risky.
